evidence_quotes.quote: Meaning there are no consumer protecting policies for games that are specifically designed to be shut down after a set period of time without the user his or her knowledge while having paid for the said service.
you don't own said product you just "lend" it and they can end it at any time for any reason.

paragraphs.text: Hello, First of all I would like to thank the EU for taking the time to open a platform to receive feedback for the Digital Fairness Act. Second I hope to whoever reads this comment will take our feedback and concerns to heart. we currently live in a time where almost everything is digital, be it for work, entertainment, education, health and wellness. Everyone giving feedback on this platform is concerned about the removal of a product they have already paid for in a market that is transformed to be almost exclusively online. Meaning there are no consumer protecting policies for games that are specifically designed to be shut down after a set period of time without the user his or her knowledge while having paid for the said service. These game developers specifically design their products this way because the minute it is shut down they don't make "enough" profit, they release the next iteration of said game which everyone would have to buy again. Simply because the first product they bought is utterly unusable. This is all while they retain the rights to said title effectively blocking non-profit third parties from setting up their own space to up to enjoy a product they have bought years ago. People who have bought a gameboy back in 1989 are still able to play their games on said device as long as the device is not physically broken. Now in 2025 we can not use a product that has just been released to the public a year prior? In my eyes thats a predatory practice, the same as why so called "lootboxes" were heavily sanctioned and moderated as far back as 2018. We are not asking companies to add lifetime support to their games, they don't have to maintain it indefinitly, they don't have to spend money on keeping servers and service online. We just want the ability to keep playing the game, the product we have paid for which are now being sold for 60 to 100 euro's excluding the paid digital content for the game which could add up in some cases to another 50 to 100 euro's. May it be by either the tools to set up our own servers. If I buy a car and the manufacturer of said brand would just take it away after I have rode it for 10000 kilometers, maybe 8000 or even 3000 depending on when I bought it. Without offering any compensation because they did not sell enough models of that car. Is that fair practice? The company is not going bankrupt and if they were I don't see why I would have to return a product that works and I have paid for. These companies are trying to imply that as soon as I put my key into the ignition, the car spontaneously combusts because they stopped offering support. Luckily I can buy the next version but instead of using a key to start the car I'd have to call them first and fill out a form of 20 pages with somewhere hidden away a line that says they can take it whenever they feel like it. This has been happening to the industry. You don't get a physical copy that contains the game. You have to connect to their online service platform otherwise you are not allowed to play and use your bought product, you can't complain about your not being able to use their product because you don't own said product you just "lend" it and they can end it at any time for any reason. Unfortunately this is the reality of what is happening world wide right now. The people gathered here giving feedback about this issue is just a small portion of the people affected by this predatory practice. I have seen feedback from Canada here, the USA, Turkey, Norway and more. It's a widespread issue and the EU is one of the only places we can still advocate for our rights. Not only for the rights as a consumer but also to safekeep and archive a part of culture. They are a piece of Mass Media just the same as movies or music. Thank you to whoever is reading this and taking the time out of their day to look at feedback of a concerned EU-Citizen but also a consumer just like you are in one way or another. Stop. Killing. Games.
